summary:
Koldihwa is an archaeological site in Allahabad district of Uttar Pradesh, India, that has attracted attention for claims of early rice cultivation dating back to approximately 7000 to 6000 BCE or even earlier. The site, along with the nearby site of Mahagara, was excavated by Indian archaeologists and yielded ceramics, stone tools, and botanical remains including charred rice grains. Initial interpretations proposed that Koldihwa represented an independent and very early center of rice domestication in South Asia.

The chronological claims for Koldihwa have been subject to significant scholarly debate. Some researchers have questioned whether the early dates represent domesticated rice or wild rice gathered by forager communities, and whether contamination or stratigraphic mixing might account for the earliest radiocarbon dates. The site nonetheless contributes to the broader picture of prehistoric South Asian communities exploiting rice — whether wild or cultivated — in the Gangetic plain during the Neolithic period.

Koldihwa is part of a growing body of South Asian evidence, including [[event:1432|Lahuradewa]], that suggests the story of rice in South Asia may be considerably more complex and locally rooted than previously assumed. Whether these sites represent independent domestication, early adoption of rice management practices, or exploitation of wild rice populations, they demonstrate that communities in the Gangetic plain were interacting with rice as a significant plant resource from very early in the Holocene.
